metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the adverse-class definition (currently `{plan-authored-post-claim, ac-strengthened, substantive-unclassified}`) with the 115b-ceea frozen label: either add `operator-attested-retag` to the adverse class set, reclassify 115b-ceea as N/A in the frozen ground truth and adjust the expected label in --verify-repro, or document the special-case rationale in the classifier rules.
evidence: Plan: 'For a reviewed ticket (had_persisted_review) that shows an adverse post-claim edit (post_claim_edit_class ∈ {plan-authored-post-claim, ac-strengthened, substantive-unclassified})'
outcome_corpus.jsonl line 40: {"ticket_id": "115b-ceea-09f8-4a93", "post_claim_edit_class": "operator-attested-retag", ...}
runs/README.md line 101: '115b-ceea ... operator-attested-retag' listed under CAUGHT-BUT-IGNORED
mine_outcome_corpus.py: _SUBSTANTIVE_CLASSES includes 'operator-attested-retag' but the plan's adverse class list explicitly omits it
findings.finding: The plan's 'Outcome classification protocol' defines adverse post-claim edits as post_claim_edit_class ∈ {plan-authored-post-claim, ac-strengthened, substantive-unclassified}, but the frozen --verify-repro case 115b-ceea is asserted to be CAUGHT-BUT-IGNORED. The live outcome_corpus.jsonl (line 40) shows 115b-ceea has post_claim_edit_class='operator-attested-retag', which is NOT in the plan's adverse class set. Under the plan's own co-located decision rules, 115b-ceea would be classified N/A (no adverse edit), making the --verify-repro assertion for that case logically unreachable with the defined classifier.
findings.location: What section — 'Outcome classification protocol'; AC2 (--verify-repro, CAUGHT-BUT-IGNORED: 115b-ceea)
scenarios: The gate_eval_instrumentation.py classifier, implemented per the plan's co-located decision rules, would produce N/A for 115b-ceea (not CAUGHT-BUT-IGNORED), causing --verify-repro to score at most 7/8, which still passes the ≥6/8 threshold but the documented 8-case ground truth is internally inconsistent.
A strict implementation that treats operator-attested-retag as non-adverse would permanently disagree with the frozen label, making the documented classification wrong by the plan's own definitions.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `operator-attested-retag` to the adverse-class enumeration in the 'Outcome classification protocol' section, consistent with its inclusion in mine_outcome_corpus.py's `_SUBSTANTIVE_CLASSES`, or reclassify 115b-ceea as UNKNOWABLE/N/A in the AC2 frozen set and update the --verify-repro expected mapping.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'substantive advisory finding' as a concrete predicate over sidecar fields — e.g. decision=="advisory" AND drop_reason is None (i.e. surfaced, not overflowed/dropped) — and document this filter rule co-located with the CAUGHT-BUT-IGNORED/MISSED classification rules in the harnesses README.
evidence: sidecar.py SURFACED_DECISIONS = ("block", "advisory") — the schema decision vocabulary
sidecar.py build_payload _slim() persists: decision, severity, priority, drop_reason, validity — but no 'substantive' field
Plan text: 'the persisted review raised ≥1 substantive advisory finding (the gate caught a concern)' — 'substantive' is used but never mapped to a field predicate
ABSENCE: nowhere in the plan or referenced files is 'substantive advisory' operationally defined as a filter condition over sidecar fields
findings.finding: The CAUGHT-BUT-IGNORED classification rule requires '≥1 substantive advisory finding' but 'substantive' is never defined as a filter predicate over the actual sidecar schema. The sidecar's build_payload stores findings with decision ∈ {"block", "advisory", "dropped", "indeterminate"} and a numeric priority field. There is no 'substantive' field. The plan's §5.2 prose reference ('did the gate say something substantive?') is not operationalized — does 'substantive advisory' mean decision=="advisory" (any advisory), or advisory AND priority ≥ some threshold, or advisory AND severity ∈ {"major", "critical"}, or advisory in SURFACED_DECISIONS (i.e., not dropped)?
findings.location: What section — 'Outcome classification protocol': 'CAUGHT-BUT-IGNORED — the persisted review raised ≥1 substantive advisory finding'
scenarios: Implementer uses decision=="advisory" (all advisories, including overflow/indeterminate); a ticket with 10 low-priority advisory overflows is classified CAUGHT-BUT-IGNORED when the gate arguably said nothing actionable.
Implementer uses priority >= 0.5 (the RECALL_MIN_PRIORITY threshold); this is never stated in the plan and creates silent inconsistency with §5.2's intent.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'substantive advisory finding' with 'surfaced advisory finding (decision=="advisory" and drop_reason is None)' throughout the classification protocol, matching the existing SURFACED_DECISIONS = ("block", "advisory") vocabulary in sidecar.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the new job reads REVIEW_RESULT blob *content* (criteria[], decision, findings per ticket) — e.g. by extending mine_outcome_corpus.py's `_CONTENT_TYPES` to include REVIEW_RESULT, or by a separate git-object walk over REVIEW_RESULT blobs — and document which approach is used and why (on-disk sidecars are capped at 50/ticket and differ from the compaction-proof git-object walk used for the outcome corpus).
evidence: mine_outcome_corpus.py line 87: _CONTENT_TYPES = {"CREATE", "STATUS", "EDIT", "COMMENT", "COMPLETION_VERDICT"} — REVIEW_RESULT is absent
mine_outcome_corpus.py lines 164–167: 'REVIEW_RESULT (and any non-content TYPE) is recorded as a {"uuid","ts"} stub (counted, not parsed)'

The plan says the job '(a) rebuilds the outcome corpus from live store history via E1's method (it imports/reuses mine_outcome_corpus.py)' and '(b) joins each reviewed ticket's outcome row to its persisted REVIEW_RESULT sidecar findings'
No specification in the plan for how REVIEW_RESULT blob content is obtained when mine_outcome_corpus.py's load_events() deliberately omits it
findings.finding: The plan states the new job 'imports/reuses mine_outcome_corpus.py, not a fork' to rebuild the outcome corpus, then also does a sidecar join reading per-finding criteria[] from REVIEW_RESULT blobs. But mine_outcome_corpus.py's _CONTENT_TYPES explicitly excludes REVIEW_RESULT — it is counted by UUID only, never blob-parsed. The plan never specifies how the new job reads REVIEW_RESULT blob *content* (criteria[], decision, findings) needed for the per-criterion join: does it extend load_events() to add REVIEW_RESULT to _CONTENT_TYPES, add a separate reader, or use on-disk sidecar files? These are architecturally incompatible choices with different retention properties.
findings.location: What section — 'it imports/reuses mine_outcome_corpus.py, not a fork'; also the sidecar join spec 'per-finding criteria[] gives the per-criterion breakdown'
scenarios: Implementer calls mine_outcome_corpus.build_corpus() verbatim and then has no REVIEW_RESULT finding content to join against — the per-criterion metrics are all NaN/zero.

Implementer reads on-disk sidecar files instead, silently truncating to the 50-event retention cap (RETAIN_PER_TICKET=50) for high-round-count tickets, producing inconsistent results vs. the git-object walk used for the outcome corpus.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in Scope specifying that gate_eval_instrumentation.py extends load_events() to add REVIEW_RESULT to _CONTENT_TYPES (so the git-object walk also parses REVIEW_RESULT blob JSON for findings/criteria[]/decision), mirroring the compaction-proof approach the outcome corpus already uses.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend `--verify-repro`) that asserts the `--emit` output contains expected schema (all known criteria keys present, ratio values ∈ [0,1], `rounds_per_ticket` distribution non-empty) using a small synthetic fixture or the 8 frozen cases to cross-check at least one computed ratio end-to-end.
evidence: "Proving command: `python ... gate_eval_instrumentation.py --emit` writes the metrics file and exits 0."
ABSENCE: no AC asserts that the emitted JSON contains expected keys, non-empty criterion entries, or plausible ratio values (e.g., blocking-FP proxy ∈ [0,1]).

"--verify-repro reproduces the §5.2 classification on the 8 frozen cases" — this tests ticket-level classification only, not the ratio aggregation arithmetic defined in 'Per-criterion trailing metrics'.
findings.finding: The `--emit` AC is satisfiable by an implementation that writes a trivially wrong or empty metrics JSON (e.g., `{}`, all-zero values, missing criteria keys), because the only assertion is 'writes the metrics file and exits 0'. The metric aggregation logic — computing advisory-application rate and blocking-FP proxy from the join — is new, non-trivial arithmetic that is not covered by `--verify-repro` (which only checks per-ticket classification, not aggregated ratios). The primary output of the job (the per-criterion metrics) thus has no correctness check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — first AC bullet (`--emit` proving command) and `--verify-repro` AC bullet
scenarios: An implementation that computes blocking-FP proxy as `len(blocking_findings) / 1` (off-by-one in denominator) would pass all ACs but produce wrong alarm thresholds.
An implementation that returns an empty metrics dict (no criteria found) would pass the `--emit` AC while silently disabling all dogfood monitoring.
A join bug that drops all matched rows (zero denominator) produces 0.0 for all ratios, passes exit-0, and looks plausible.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `--verify-repro` to also run `--emit` over the 8 frozen-case fixture and assert that (a) the output JSON contains at least the criteria exercised by those 8 cases, (b) blocking-FP proxy and advisory-application rate for those criteria fall within expected ranges (or equal hand-computed values from the 8 cases). This keeps the test hermetic and exercises the full aggregation path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the UNKNOWABLE rule with the 3006-e198 frozen case: either expand the rule to cover 'ac-strengthened with confound' (if that is the intended generalization), or document that UNKNOWABLE additionally includes cases where human §5.2 adjudication determined latency vs blindness could not be separated regardless of the mechanical class, and annotate 3006-e198 as a hardcoded exception in the --verify-repro fixture.
evidence: Plan: 'UNKNOWABLE — no persisted review, or the edit is substantive-unclassified with a confound (force_close OR reopen_count>0)'
runs/README.md §5 re-verification table: '1 UNKNOWABLE case | 3006-e198 | present; ac-strengthened' — the corpus class is ac-strengthened, not substantive-unclassified
Plan: 'The rules are deterministic and documented in the job's README'
findings.finding: The plan presents the UNKNOWABLE classification rule as 'deterministic' ('substantive-unclassified with a confound — force_close OR reopen_count>0'), but the one UNKNOWABLE frozen case (3006-e198) has post_claim_edit_class='ac-strengthened' in the outcome corpus (confirmed by the runs/README.md §5 re-verification table), not 'substantive-unclassified'. The stated deterministic rule cannot reproduce UNKNOWABLE for this case from corpus fields alone, contradicting the plan's assertion that the 8-case reproduction is fully mechanical. The ≥6/8 slack in AC2 is wide enough to tolerate this one miss, but the plan document and harnesses/README documentation would incorrectly claim the rules are fully deterministic when they require human semantic judgment for at least one case.
findings.location: What section — 'Outcome classification protocol'; AC2 — '--verify-repro reproduces ... UNKNOWABLE: 3006-e198'
scenarios: The harnesses README documents a fully-deterministic classifier that cannot actually reproduce the UNKNOWABLE label for 3006-e198 from corpus fields, confusing future maintainers who try to re-derive it.
If a future calibration adds a 4th UNKNOWABLE case with ac-strengthened class and a confound, the documented rule would silently misclassify it as MISSED or N/A.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a hardcoded UNKNOWABLE annotation for 3006-e198 in the --verify-repro fixture (the §5.2 human judgment that this was UNKNOWABLE overrides the mechanical class), and document in the README that the UNKNOWABLE rule has one hardcoded historical exception whose human reasoning is recorded in §5.2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a verification step to AC1 (or a new AC) that runs `--emit --window 50` (or any non-default N) and asserts the output metrics JSON reflects only the N most-recent reviews, so the `--window N` flag described in Scope is covered by an acceptance criterion.
evidence: Scope lists: '--verify-repro (8-case reproduction, asserts ≥6/8), --emit (writes the per-criterion metrics JSON), --window N'
AC1 proving command: 'python docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/gate_eval_instrumentation.py --emit' — no --window argument
findings.finding: The `--window N` flag is explicitly described as a supported parameter in Scope ('--window N') but no Acceptance Criterion verifies that the window parameter is functional — AC1's proving command is `--emit` with no `--window` argument, leaving the parameterization untested by the AC set.
findings.location: Scope section (gate_eval_instrumentation.py flags list) vs. AC1 proving command
scenarios: An implementer ships --window N without a test; a future caller passes --window 50 and gets silent wrong output (e.g., defaults to 200) with no AC catching the regression.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1's proving command to include a second invocation: `python ... gate_eval_instrumentation.py --emit --window 50` and assert the output file contains a `window` field equal to 50 (or that row-count ≤ 50).

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the output path for `--emit`, enumerate the consumers of that file, and document the overwrite/retirement policy (e.g., 'idempotent overwrite; previous file replaced in-place; no archival').
evidence: Plan says `--emit` 'writes the per-criterion metrics JSON' but never names the output path.
Consumers are described only as 'the epic's dogfood monitoring consumes' without naming specific jobs, dashboards, or files.
No statement about whether old output files are overwritten, archived, or retained — accumulation vs. idempotent overwrite is unstated.
findings.finding: The metrics JSON output file written by `--emit` has no specified path, no enumeration of which downstream consumers read it, and no stated retirement or accumulation policy — the full T9 lifecycle (CREATE→CONSUME→RETIRE) is incomplete for this artifact.
findings.location: Scope section / AC line 1 (`--emit` writes the metrics file)
scenarios: A downstream monitoring consumer hard-codes the wrong path because the spec never defined it, silently reading stale data.
Two operators run `--emit` concurrently (e.g., scheduled + manual), producing a torn/partial metrics file with no protection.
After the epic ends, the metrics file accumulates historical runs indefinitely (or is never cleaned up) with no policy.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope/AC section: output path (e.g., `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/metrics/gate_eval_metrics.json`), a note that each `--emit` run atomically overwrites the previous output (tmp-file rename), and list the specific downstream consumer(s) by name.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the --emit output file path and JSON schema (at minimum: top-level structure, per-criterion record keys for advisory-application-rate, blocking-fp-proxy, rounds-per-ticket, and the window N used) in the harnesses README or Scope section, so the output is machine-readable by downstream consumers without inspecting the implementation.
evidence: AC1 proving command only verifies exit 0 — no assertion about schema, path, or content correctness
Plan names three metric types (advisory-application rate, blocking-FP proxy, rounds/ticket) but gives no JSON key names for them
ABSENCE: no example output, no schema block, no output path constant analogous to mine_outcome_corpus.py's OUT_PATH
findings.finding: The `--emit` metrics output schema is referenced but never defined. The plan says the job 'emits per-criterion trailing metrics (advisory-application rate, blocking-FP proxy, rounds/ticket) to a JSON output' but specifies no output path, no top-level key names, no per-criterion record structure, and no field types. Downstream consumers (the 'dogfood monitoring' the plan says these metrics feed) need an exact schema to read the file. This is a schema/output-format reference without a vocabulary.
findings.location: AC1: 'python … gate_eval_instrumentation.py --emit writes the metrics file and exits 0'; Scope: 'writes the per-criterion metrics JSON'
scenarios: Two implementers produce incompatible JSON schemas (e.g., flat per-criterion vs. nested); the downstream monitoring consumer breaks on one.
The output file path is not specified, so a downstream consumer cannot locate it without reading source code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a minimum blocking-decisions denominator (e.g., n≥20) to the blocking-FP proxy demotion rule, below which the metric is reported but the alarm does not fire, and document this floor in the harnesses README and docs/plan-review-gate.md playbook.
evidence: Plan: 'blocking-FP proxy(C) = ... / (# tickets where C raised ≥1 blocking finding)' — no minimum denominator specified
Plan: 'When a criterion's trailing blocking-FP proxy exceeds 10%, demote it'
T5e demotion was grounded in n=901 findings; the plan sets no analogous floor for the per-criterion denominator in the new metric
findings.finding: The blocking-FP proxy demotion trigger ('>10% over the trailing N=200 window') has no minimum-sample denominator guard. A criterion that blocks infrequently (e.g., 3 blocking decisions in the window) would trigger demotion from a single force-close event (1/3 ≈ 33%), a statistically meaningless signal. Given that the plan's own precedent (T5e demotion) was based on n=901 findings, the absence of a minimum-n guard on the per-criterion blocking-FP denominator risks premature demotion of low-volume criteria from noise.
findings.location: What section — 'Blocking-FP proxy(C)' definition; 'Alarm / demotion rule' section
scenarios: A newly-promoted blocking criterion with only 4 blocking decisions in the trailing window gets demoted after 1 author force-closes a ticket, even though 4 data points are statistically insufficient.
An advisory criterion promoted to blocking mid-window will have a small denominator for its entire first window, making it trivially demote-eligible from noise.
findings.suggested_fix: In the metric definition: 'The demotion alarm fires only when the denominator (# tickets with ≥1 blocking finding in the window) ≥ 20; below this floor, the metric is reported as low-data and the alarm is suppressed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document whether the 'no corresponding adverse post-claim edit' leg of the blocking-FP proxy is intentionally conservative (known overestimate) or whether a further condition (e.g., excluding completion_verifier_fail_count==0 tickets) is intended to filter valid-block cases, and add this rationale to the classifier rules in the harnesses README.
evidence: Plan text: 'proxied by force_close OR reopen_count>0 OR no corresponding adverse post-claim edit'
A ticket with a valid block, no post-claim edit (author fixed it pre-claim), reopen_count=0, force_close=False passes this condition and is counted as a blocking FP
ABSENCE: no disambiguation rule for the 'no adverse post-claim edit' leg of the disjunction
findings.finding: The blocking-FP proxy denominator uses 'proxied by force_close OR reopen_count>0 OR no corresponding adverse post-claim edit' as the not-block-worthy signal. The third condition ('no corresponding adverse post-claim edit') treats any ticket where the author did NOT make a post-claim substantive edit as evidence the block was wrong — but this conflates (a) valid blocks where the author fixed the issue before closing (no edit needed) with (b) FP blocks that weren't needed. No fallback or disambiguation rule is provided for this ambiguity. The plan does not specify what to do when this condition fires but the ticket also had a clean completion-verifier pass (which might indicate the block was valid and the author remediated correctly).
findings.location: What section — 'blocking-FP proxy(C)' definition: 'proxied by force_close OR reopen_count>0 OR no corresponding adverse post-claim edit'
scenarios: A criterion correctly blocks a ticket; the author fixes the plan before claiming (pre-claim edit, outside the post-claim window), then closes cleanly — counted as a blocking FP, inflating the FP proxy and potentially triggering spurious demotion.
A criterion blocks a ticket with a valid structural gap; the author closes without editing (perhaps the gap was minor); it is counted as a blocking FP.

findings.finding: The plan's alarm rule ('blocking-FP proxy >10% → demote criterion to advisory') conflates true FPs with legitimate blocks that the author bypassed (force_close, reopen without plan change). The proxy cannot distinguish an override-of-FP (gate should not have blocked) from an override-of-TP (gate correctly blocked; author circumvented it) — yet the plan uses this signal to make a demotion decision with the same logic as the T3/T10 precedent, which was grounded in adjudication-corpus TP/FP labels, not the outcome proxy.
findings.location: What section — alarm rule; 'precedent: T3/T10'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Surface the 10% blocking-FP threshold as a named constant (e.g. `FP_DEMOTION_THRESHOLD = 0.10`) in the instrumentation job, and/or a `--fp-threshold` CLI flag, so the playbook and code share a single authoritative value and operators can adjust without editing production logic.
evidence: 'When a criterion's trailing blocking-FP proxy exceeds 10%' — threshold stated as a fixed value with no mention of a CLI flag or named config constant
'--window N' is made configurable but no equivalent '--fp-threshold' or named constant is mentioned for the 10% rule
'Precedent for demotion on FP evidence: T5e' implies this threshold may be revisited as the system matures
findings.finding: The 10% blocking-FP-proxy demotion threshold is a business rule expected to evolve (calibrated from the Tricorder baseline) but is not surfaced as a configurable parameter — it appears hardcoded in the instrumentation job logic and only documented in the playbook. An operator wanting to adjust it must edit both code and documentation with no single authoritative config location.
findings.location: ## What — 'Alarm / demotion rule' section; `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/gate_eval_instrumentation.py`; `docs/plan-review-gate.md`
scenarios: As the gate matures and FP rates shift, the team wants to tighten the threshold to 8% — they must grep for '0.10'/'10%' across both the Python job and the markdown playbook with no single source of truth.
A future operator reads only the playbook and sets 10% as the alarm, unaware the code uses a different hardcoded value if they diverge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `FP_DEMOTION_THRESHOLD = 0.10` as a module-level named constant in `gate_eval_instrumentation.py`, reference it by name in the metric computation, and cite it by name in the playbook doc (e.g. 'see `FP_DEMOTION_THRESHOLD` in the harness'). Optionally expose as `--fp-threshold` CLI arg defaulting to that constant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the rebuild writes to `runs/outcome_corpus.jsonl` (overwriting E1's frozen file) or to a separate working path, and document ownership: who may update `runs/outcome_corpus.jsonl` and under what conditions.
evidence: '(a) rebuilds the outcome corpus from live store history via E1's method' — no output path stated for the rebuild step.
E1 is described as having 'frozen the per-ticket outcome corpus (`runs/outcome_corpus.jsonl`, 628 rows)'
If the reuse of `mine_outcome_corpus.py` writes to the same path, the frozen artifact is mutated on every R7 run.
findings.finding: The plan states the job 'rebuilds the outcome corpus from live store history via E1's method' but does not specify whether this rebuild writes back to `runs/outcome_corpus.jsonl` (the E1-owned frozen artifact) or to a separate path — creating an ambiguous ownership boundary where R7 may silently overwrite E1's frozen corpus.
findings.location: What section: 'rebuilds the outcome corpus from live store history via E1's method (it imports/reuses `mine_outcome_corpus.py`, not a fork)'
scenarios: An operator runs `gate_eval_instrumentation.py --emit` and inadvertently overwrites `runs/outcome_corpus.jsonl`, replacing E1's frozen 628-row snapshot with a newer corpus that changes the `--verify-repro` baseline, causing previously-passing repro checks to fail or give different results.
findings.suggested_fix: Specify that `gate_eval_instrumentation.py` reads from `runs/outcome_corpus.jsonl` as a read-only input and writes a *separate* rebuilt corpus to a temp or versioned path (e.g., `runs/outcome_corpus_latest.jsonl`), or explicitly state that overwriting the frozen file is intentional and that `--verify-repro` always pins to the 8 frozen case IDs regardless.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `gate_eval_instrumentation.py` calls `from rebar._store.fsutil import atomic_write` for its metrics-file write rather than copy-pasting a third `_atomic_write_jsonl` helper alongside the two already in the harnesses directory.
evidence: mine_outcome_corpus.py line 389: `def _atomic_write_jsonl(rows, path)` — identical temp+replace pattern
build_adjudication_corpus.py line 190: `def _atomic_write_jsonl(rows, path)` — exact same function body copied
src/rebar/_store/fsutil.py:48: `def atomic_write(...)` — canonical implementation, already importable from harnesses
build_adjudication_corpus.py line 49: `from rebar.llm.plan_review import sidecar` — proves rebar is importable from the harness dir
findings.finding: Both existing harness scripts in the same directory (`mine_outcome_corpus.py:389` and `build_adjudication_corpus.py:190`) define an identical `_atomic_write_jsonl` private helper (12-line temp-file + os.replace pattern). The new `gate_eval_instrumentation.py` will likely introduce a third copy when it writes its `--emit` metrics JSON. Meanwhile `rebar._store.fsutil.atomic_write` — the canonical single-source implementation of this exact pattern — is already importable from these harnesses (build_adjudication_corpus already imports from `rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar`, proving the rebar package is on the path).
findings.location: docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/gate_eval_instrumentation.py (planned) — compared to mine_outcome_corpus.py:389 and build_adjudication_corpus.py:190
scenarios: Developer implementing gate_eval_instrumentation.py copies the pattern a third time, leaving three diverging copies of the same crash-atomicity logic in one directory.
A bug fix to the temp-file cleanup logic (e.g. the `finally: if os.path.exists(tmp): os.remove(tmp)` race) must be applied three places instead of one.
findings.suggested_fix: In gate_eval_instrumentation.py, replace any inline `_atomic_write_jsonl` with `from rebar._store.fsutil import atomic_write` and call `atomic_write(path, json.dumps(metrics, indent=2) + '\n')` — same one-liner already used across a dozen production sites.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the leaf intentionally replaces the parent's 'append a gate-eval record (sidecar-style, reducer-ignored) on each material post-claim edit' write-path with a purely offline batch job — if so, update the parent plan to reflect this scope change first (per the CONFLICT RULE the parent wins); if the write-path is still intended, add it to the leaf's Scope and ACs.
evidence: Parent: 'on every material post-claim description edit, link the edit to the ticket's latest REVIEW_RESULT and append a gate-eval record (sidecar-style, reducer-ignored) classifying it MISSED / CAUGHT-BUT-IGNORED / UNKNOWABLE'
Leaf Scope: 'No change to src/rebar/ — no gate or source behaviour changes.'
Leaf What: 'a re-runnable gate-eval instrumentation job' — framed entirely as an offline read/report job with no write-back to the store.
ABSENCE: no leaf AC or scope item mentions writing gate-eval records to any sidecar or event log in the tickets store.
findings.finding: The parent R7 plan specifies that the job should 'append a gate-eval record (sidecar-style, reducer-ignored)' on each material post-claim edit — a persistent write-back path into the store that makes outcomes durable and queryable by future tooling. The leaf plan omits this write-path entirely, delivering only a read-only offline batch job. This is not a consistent narrowing: the parent's central architectural choice (an event-log write path per qualifying edit) is absent from the leaf's scope.
findings.location: Parent R7 §What ('append a gate-eval record (sidecar-style, reducer-ignored)') vs. Leaf Scope section ('No change to src/rebar/' + 'docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/gate_eval_instrumentation.py')
scenarios: The parent's downstream consumers (R1–R6) expect gate-eval records to accumulate automatically in the store as edits happen; if the write-path is absent, the corpus stays frozen at whatever mine_outcome_corpus.py last produced and does not self-update.

The monthly reporting cadence the parent describes ('Report monthly') implicitly requires a running write path; without it, every 'run' must re-mine from git objects rather than reading incrementally from appended records.

findings.finding: The outcome classification enum the new job must use — MISSED / CAUGHT-BUT-IGNORED / UNKNOWABLE (from the R7 row of the research doc) — is not defined as a co-located decision protocol. The plan says to use 'the E1 outcome-corpus method,' but E1 (mine_outcome_corpus.py) classifies description-edit class (post_claim_edit_class), not gate-eval outcome class. No decision rules for MISSED vs CAUGHT-BUT-IGNORED vs UNKNOWABLE are co-located with the new job's specification.
findings.location: What section ('classifies each reviewed ticket's outcome from store history (the E1 outcome-corpus method)'); Acceptance Criteria AC2 ('classifies the 8 §5.2 tickets … against the E1 frozen labels')

findings.finding: The 'per-criterion advisory-application rate' metric is named in the What section as a metric the job must emit, but no definition exists: how it is derived from the outcome corpus (which has no per-criterion REVIEW_RESULT breakdown — only aggregate review_round_count) is unspecified, and no join key to the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar corpus is defined.
findings.location: What section ('per-criterion advisory-application rate, blocking-FP proxy, rounds/ticket')

findings.finding: The plan invokes 'precedent: T3/T10' as evidence that the alarm-rule response (blocking-FP proxy >10% → demote criterion to advisory) has a grounded precedent in the codebase. However, both T3 and T10 in criteria_routing.json have always carried `default_posture: advisory` and `block_threshold: 0.95` — they were never blocking criteria that were subsequently demoted. docs/plan-review-gate.md describes them as 'confident-but-routinely-ignored' advisory criteria, not as prior demotion examples. No calibration record (calibrations 1–3 documented in plan-review-gate.md) shows T3 or T10 ever promoted to blocking then demoted. The precedent claim is therefore unverifiable from the codebase.
findings.location: ## What — 'documented alarm rule: trailing blocking-FP proxy >10% -> demote that criterion to advisory (precedent: T3/T10)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change '(precedent: T3/T10)' to '(precedent: T5e — demoted in calibration 3: validity 0.391, 59% verifier-drop rate, surviving p90 priority 0.27; see docs/plan-review-gate.md §Scope)'. T5e is the only criterion in the calibration record that was actually demoted from a blocking-eligible position to advisory.

findings.finding: The plan claims 'precedent: T3/T10' for the alarm-rule response of demoting a criterion from blocking to advisory, but T3 and T10 are documented as advisory by design from their introduction (default_posture='advisory', block_threshold=0.95 in criteria_v8.json) — no demotion event exists to cite. The cited 'precedent' is unverified and potentially fabricated; the playbook response has never actually been exercised.
findings.location: What section: 'Documented alarm rule: trailing blocking-FP proxy >10% -> demote that criterion to advisory (precedent: T3/T10)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(precedent: T3/T10)' with '(first exercise of this rule — T3 and T10 are advisory by design, not demoted; this rule fires when a criterion configured as blocking accumulates >10% blocked-FP rate in the trailing window)' to accurately represent the history and prevent the playbook from being misleading.

findings.finding: The leaf's reproduction gate validates against 'E1 frozen labels', but the parent's acceptance condition says 'agreement against this report's human-judged labels' (the §5.2 human classification in task-decomposition-sota-2026.md). The E1 outcome corpus (outcome_corpus.jsonl) is documented in runs/README.md as a mechanical, algorithm-derived classification, not a human-adjudicated TP/FP label set (the human-adjudicated finding labels were explicitly descoped). Validating against the mechanical E1 corpus rather than the parent's §5.2 human judgments may satisfy a weaker criterion than the parent intended.
findings.location: Leaf AC2: 'the job classifies the 8 §5.2 tickets with >=6/8 agreement against the E1 frozen labels'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the E1 frozen labels' with 'the §5.2 human-judged labels (MISSED/CAUGHT-BUT-IGNORED/UNKNOWABLE) as recorded in task-decomposition-sota-2026.md §5.2 and the eight case-ticket ids therein'.

findings.finding: AC3 names 'docs/plan-review-gate.md' as the target file for the alarm rule and playbook, but the Scope section targets 'docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/' (a directory). These conflict: an executor cannot know whether to create a top-level file or a file within the experiments subdirectory.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC3 vs. Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Update AC3 to read: 'The alarm rule (trailing blocking-FP proxy >10% -> demote to advisory) and response playbook are documented in docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/README.md.'

findings.finding: AC3 names 'docs/plan-review-gate.md' as the artifact to verify against, but Scope specifies 'docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/' (a directory with a README). These are different paths/artifact types; an implementer verifying AC3 would look in a different place than what Scope directs them to build.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC3 vs. Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC3 to: 'The alarm rule and response playbook are documented in docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/README.md (or plan-review-gate.md within that directory).'

findings.finding: AC3 names 'docs/plan-review-gate.md' as the file where the alarm rule and response playbook must be documented, but the Scope section names 'docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/' (a directory with a README) as the only edit target. These are different, pre-existing artifacts: docs/plan-review-gate.md is a large gate-design document, while docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/README.md is the instrumentation writeup home. An executor cannot determine which file to write to.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC3 vs. Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC3 to: 'The alarm rule (trailing blocking-FP proxy >10% -> demote to advisory) and response playbook are documented in docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/README.md.' and ensure the Scope bullet already covers this file.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
